session_title: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction

extractions.session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
extractions.speaker: Kaja Kallas
extractions.extraction: The opposite of a rules-based order is tyranny and war, and that is where we are now; the current crisis is an opportunity to develop international law further, as has happened after previous global crises.
extractions.context: Kallas drew a historical line from the League of Nations through the UN, arguing each breakdown generated reform; she cited the current proliferation of wars as evidence of failure.

extractions.session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
extractions.speaker: Kaja Kallas
extractions.extraction: The core failure of the current multilateral system is accountability: states sign up to the UN Charter but there is no functioning mechanism to hold even the most powerful actors to account when they breach its rules.
extractions.context: Kallas pointed to the dysfunctional Security Council and argued that a single leader can order wars that kill thousands with no personal consequence.

extractions.session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
extractions.speaker: Prince Faisal bin Farhan Al Saud
extractions.extraction: The breakdown of the rules-based order is not a new revelation for the West — it has long been the lived reality for countries in the developing world, making the current European alarm a belated recognition rather than a genuine turning point.
extractions.context: Prompted by moderator's question about whether the order-change debate is a Eurocentric perspective; Faisal argued that 'might makes right' predated Ukraine for most of the world and cited conflicts in the Middle East handled outside UN mandates.

extractions.session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
extractions.speaker: Prince Faisal bin Farhan Al Saud
extractions.extraction: The fact that even European defenders of the old system are now admitting it was broken makes this moment more honest and, counterintuitively, more optimistic than previous years.
extractions.context: Faisal said he is more optimistic than last year precisely because frank conversations are now happening across all blocs, including among former ardent supporters of the old system.

extractions.session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
extractions.speaker: Pedro Arnulfo Sánchez Suárez
extractions.extraction: Misinformation and disinformation is already the second-greatest global risk after interstate conflict and may soon become the greatest, as AI accelerates its reach — a claim Colombia's experience with the Trump administration over drug policy illustrates.
extractions.context: Sánchez described how disinformation convinced President Trump that Colombia was doing nothing on drugs, and cited a global risk ranking for the broader claim. [word uncertain in source]

extractions.session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
extractions.speaker: Pedro Arnulfo Sánchez Suárez
extractions.extraction: Colombia is the world's best ally in the fight against transnational organized crime, and the drug trade cannot be eliminated while global demand for drugs — currently involving 316 million consumers — remains unaddressed.
extractions.context: Sánchez offered this after the moderator pressed him on whether the US asserting dominance in its hemisphere was acceptable; he reframed the US-Colombia relationship around shared counter-narcotics interest.

extractions.session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
extractions.speaker: Michael Waltz
extractions.extraction: The Trump administration is not abandoning multilateralism but reforming it: the UN has already agreed to its first-ever 15% budget cut, nearly 3,000 headquarters staff will be cut, and the goal is to return the institution to its core peacekeeping mandate.
extractions.context: Waltz cited the December budget agreement and drew an analogy to NATO, arguing Trump's pressure on burden-sharing made NATO stronger and would do the same for the UN.

extractions.session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
extractions.speaker: Michael Waltz
extractions.extraction: The world was already at the brink 18 months ago — multiple active wars, Iran racing for a nuclear weapon, the Houthis closing the Suez Canal, a massive South American refugee crisis — and US leadership has pulled it back from that edge.
extractions.context: Waltz directly challenged the panel's framing ('The World on the Brink') by arguing the brink was the prior status quo, not the current moment.

extractions.session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
extractions.speaker: Michael Waltz
extractions.extraction: The Board of Peace, established by unanimous UN Security Council resolution, represents 'focused multilateralism' — a legitimate new instrument backed by Muslim-majority nations and Gulf Arab states, with billions in funding and 8,000 troops pledged by Indonesia.
extractions.context: Waltz responded to moderator and audience challenges that the Board of Peace's White House charter does not mention Gaza and exceeds its UN mandate; he cited Indonesia's troop pledge and the Sharm el-Sheikh meeting.

extractions.session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
extractions.speaker: Kaja Kallas
extractions.extraction: The Board of Peace as constituted does not reflect the UN Security Council resolution that authorized it: the resolution limited the board's mandate to Gaza until 2027 and required Palestinian participation, neither of which appears in the White House charter.
extractions.context: Kallas pushed back directly on Waltz's claim that the Board of Peace was an outgrowth of the UN resolution, citing specific omissions in the charter document.

extractions.session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
extractions.speaker: Kaja Kallas
extractions.extraction: Any reformed multilateral system must preserve equality of states and the principle that no country — including the most powerful — is above the law; without those two elements, reform is just the substitution of one form of domination for another.
extractions.context: Kallas made this in response to Waltz's reform agenda, explicitly connecting it to Prince Faisal's earlier call for a system that reflects today's world.

extractions.session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
extractions.speaker: Michael Waltz
extractions.extraction: It is fair for the United States to demand burden-sharing reform because the US has historically paid more than 180 countries combined into the UN system, and with $37 trillion in debt that level of subsidy is no longer sustainable.
extractions.context: Waltz responded to the Polish audience member's question about whether the US is withdrawing from the rules-based order; he framed the demand for reform as accountability to American taxpayers.

extractions.session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
extractions.speaker: Kaja Kallas
extractions.extraction: Europe is not a free-rider: when the US goes to war, European countries go alongside it and lose their own people, and European development aid constitutes the largest share of global contributions — making the narrative of one-sided American underwriting incomplete.
extractions.context: Kallas pushed back on Waltz's burden-sharing argument by pointing to EU development aid volumes and noting that US military power is magnified precisely because it has allies, unlike Russia.

extractions.session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
extractions.speaker: Kaja Kallas
extractions.extraction: The key differentiator between the United States and other great powers like China or Russia is the US alliance network; undermining those alliances therefore undermines the very source of American primacy.
extractions.context: Kallas made this argument in response to the question of whether the US needs the rules-based order, framing allies as the structural reason US power exceeds that of comparable-sized rivals.

extractions.session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
extractions.speaker: Audience Member 3
extractions.extraction: If the Board of Peace includes Israel's Prime Minister but no Palestinian representative, in what sense can it be called a peace process rather than a mechanism that exempts one party from accountability?
extractions.context: Mustafa Barghouti from Palestine raised this from the floor; Kallas responded on accountability in general terms and Prince Faisal acknowledged the concern but neither directly answered whether Palestinian exclusion undermined the board's legitimacy.

extractions.session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
extractions.speaker: Prince Faisal bin Farhan Al Saud
extractions.extraction: A ceasefire that stops the killing but not the dying — because aid access remains blocked — is not a real ceasefire, and stabilizing Gaza, maintaining the unity of Gaza and the West Bank, and then advancing Palestinian self-determination must be treated as a sequential but linked process.
extractions.context: Prince Faisal responded to the Palestinian audience member's question about accountability for ceasefire violations and to Waltz's claims of progress; he acknowledged ongoing aid access problems while defending continued Saudi engagement.

extractions.session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
extractions.extraction: Does the Trump administration believe in a multilateral system whose rules would constrain the United States itself, or does it advocate only for selective, US-led coalitions that operate outside binding multilateral constraints?
extractions.context: The moderator asked this directly (turn 26); Waltz responded by pointing to results — NATO, Gaza, Azerbaijan-Armenia — without affirming that the US accepts rules-based constraints on its own conduct. The tension was never resolved.

extractions.session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
extractions.extraction: If the rules-based order has always been experienced as 'might makes right' by the Global South, what concrete difference would a reformed system make for developing countries compared to the current transition toward explicit great-power spheres of influence?
extractions.context: Prince Faisal established that the developing world has long lived under the de facto breakdown of rules (turns 3 and 5), but neither he nor other panelists articulated what a reformed system would specifically change for those countries.

extractions.session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
extractions.speaker: Prince Faisal bin Farhan Al Saud
extractions.extraction: The transition period between the old order and whatever replaces it is inherently dangerous, and economic inequality amplified by technological change is a major risk factor during that dislocation — though the technology may eventually deliver net benefits.
extractions.context: Faisal answered the closing question about whether the world is becoming more dangerous; he situated current instability as transition-phase risk rather than permanent deterioration. [word uncertain in source]
